title: Open Source HBIM and OpenAI: Review and New Analyses on LLMs Integration
contribution: LLM通过生成Python脚本实现了文本到BIM的工作流，为开源HBIM提供了自动化的参数化建模方案
abstract: This work concentrates on an experimental project for the integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) inside a Historic Building Information Modeling (HBIM) workflow. In particular, this evaluation was carried out by using open source solutions as concerns parametric modeling of BIM elements. This experimental test focuses on how Python scripts, generated by AI agents, can create parametric models for HBIM purposes and archaeology: starting from the archaeological plan, the parametric modeling of the Parthenon temple was carried out via a text-to-BIM workflow based on OpenAI and open source tools. The use of AI in generating these scripts can potentially automate and streamline the modeling process, making it more efficient and less prone to human error (or almost). FreeCAD, being a Python-based software, is identified as the perfect fieldwork for this test. Its open source nature allows extensive customization and experimentation, making it an ideal platform for integrating AI-generated Python scripts. In addition to proving a flexible and operative BIM platform, this approach could achieve the same results by parametric modeling via Python scripts generated by LLMs. By harnessing the power of LLMs, FreeCAD could serve not only as a robust BIM tool but also as a testbed for pushing the boundaries of what AI can achieve in the realm of parametric modeling and HBIM. This project opens new possibilities for automating the creation of detailed, accurate BIM models, ultimately contributing to the preservation and management of heritage buildings.
methodology: A text-to-BIM workflow is proposed where GPT-4 Turbo (accessed via Microsoft Copilot) generates Python scripts that are imported into FreeCAD (v0.21.1) to create parametric models. The process involves three phases: (1) Copilot investigation and mini-training on FreeCAD capabilities and Parthenon temple architecture using the archaeological plan as input; (2) iterative text-to-BIM modeling where architectural requests are sent to Copilot, which returns Python scripts using DRAFT, SKETCHER, and PART workbenches to generate parametric objects; (3) IFC classification of modeled elements (columns as ifcColumn, Naos as ifcWall) via the ARCH workbench and ifcOpenShell library invoked by Copilot-generated scripts.
results: Copilot successfully generated Python scripts for most Parthenon elements including the peristyle columns (8x17 Doric columns), capitals (abacus and echinus), Naos/Cella structure with openings, inner columns, architraves, and frieze. However, the process required multiple iterative corrections for metric errors, positioning issues, and architectural knowledge gaps. The sloped roof and pediment could not be generated by Copilot after 13 attempts and had to be manually modeled. IFC classification of columns and walls was successfully achieved via AI-generated scripts. The study concludes that while text-to-BIM via LLMs shows promise for automating HBIM workflows, current limitations in architectural comprehension and coding accuracy prevent full automation, requiring human oversight and verification.
pdfFile: Diara etal - 2025 - Open Source HBIM and OpenAI Review and New Analyses on LLMs Integration.pdf
